_work.title: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
_work.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
_work.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection), per item. Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The twelve emblematic 'keys' of the *Duodecim Claves* attributed to Basil Valentine — each a dense alchemical riddle staging one step of the Great Work, from the antimonial purgation of the king to the final fixed Stone. Engraved by Michael Maier for the *Tripus Aureus* (1618) and reissued in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).

entries.title: The Seventh Key of Basil Valentine (*Septième Clef* / *Clavis VII*)
entries.work: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The seventh of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Merian engraving of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) after Michael Maier — the key of right measure and the secret salt.

## Iconography
The plate sets its emblem within a furnished alchemical chamber, the work weighed and proportioned by figures who measure out the matter; the older keys read this stage as the balancing of the volatile against the fixed, the salt added that the body may stand. As in the rest of the series, marginal devices and an inscribed motto carry the riddle the picture only half-shows.

## Significance
Valentine's sequence walks the [[motif:philosophers stone|opus]] from the antimonial purging of the king through [[motif:coniunctio|conjunction]] toward the fixed Stone; the seventh key turns on weight, salt and the right ordering of the parts — the alchemist made registrar of his own vessel. The *Duodecim Claves* circulated first in German (1599), then in Maier's Latin *Tripus Aureus* (1618), and it is the *Musaeum Hermeticum* recension that fixed these images in the learned memory.

## For artists & game designers
A 'calibration' beat: the recipe will not advance until the player balances two opposed reagents on a scale — overshoot and the brew is spoiled.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *Duodecim Claves*, in Michael Maier's *Tripus Aureus* (1618); engr. M. Merian in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).

entries.title: The Eighth Key of Basil Valentine (*Huitième Clef* / *Clavis VIII*)
entries.work: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The eighth of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Merian engraving of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) — the key of death, sowing and rising again.

## Iconography
The emblem turns on the parable of the buried seed: a sower or a corpse laid in the earth, the dead grain that must rot before it can quicken. The accompanying scene stages a burial and a sprouting, the black stage of the work shown as a planting that promises harvest.

## Significance
The eighth key is the *putrefactio* made gospel — 'unless the grain die.' In Valentine's scheme the matter must blacken and corrupt before the white and red can follow; the [[motif:death|death]] of the body is the necessary gate to its glorified return. This sacramental reading of decay, half-chemistry and half-sermon, is characteristic of the German alchemical piety that the *Duodecim Claves* carried into the *Musaeum Hermeticum*.

## For artists & game designers
The 'plant to harvest' beat: the player must deliberately let a reagent rot — accept the loss — to unlock a stronger regrown form.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *Duodecim Claves*, in Michael Maier's *Tripus Aureus* (1618); engr. M. Merian in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).

entries.title: The Ninth Key of Basil Valentine (*Neuvième Clef* / *Clavis IX*)
entries.work: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The ninth of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Merian engraving of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) — the key of heavenly dew and the nourishing of the stone.

## Iconography
The emblem draws an influence down from above — rays, falling dew, a fountain or vessel that gathers the celestial moisture — to feed the matter quickening below. The composition links the upper heaven to the lower flask, the macrocosm pouring its virtue into the alchemist's glass.

## Significance
Here the work is fed by what the philosophers called the dew or *aqua vitae* of the philosophers — the spirit drawn from above to reanimate the purified body. The ninth key belongs to the conviction, central to Valentine and to the whole German-lands tradition, that the [[motif:philosophers stone|stone]] grows only when heaven's influence is captured and married to a properly prepared earth.

## For artists & game designers
A 'collect the dew' beat: the player must time an action to a celestial event — a conjunction, a dawn — to harvest a resource available only then.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *Duodecim Claves*, in Michael Maier's *Tripus Aureus* (1618); engr. M. Merian in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).

entries.title: The Tenth Key of Basil Valentine (*Dixième Clef* / *Clavis X*)
entries.work: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The tenth of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Merian engraving of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) — the key of the wrestling dragon and eagle, the volatile bound to the fixed.

## Iconography
A dragon and a soaring eagle are set against one another, the creeping earthbound serpent and the heaven-climbing bird — the alchemist's standing shorthand for the fixed body and the volatile spirit that must be made to hold together. The plate shows the contest by which the volatile is repeatedly raised and returned until it consents to stay.

## Significance
The [[motif:dragon|dragon]]-and-[[motif:eagle|eagle]] pair is one of the oldest emblems of *sublimation* and *fixation*: the eagle bears the dragon up, the dragon weighs the eagle down, and the Stone is the truce between them. The tenth key marks the near-completion of the work, where what was driven off is at last fixed into the abiding tincture.

## For artists & game designers
The 'bind the volatile' beat: a fast, escaping element (the eagle) must be chained to a heavy stable one (the dragon) before it flees the vessel for good.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *Duodecim Claves*, in Michael Maier's *Tripus Aureus* (1618); engr. M. Merian in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).

entries.title: The Eleventh Key of Basil Valentine (*Onzième Clef* / *Clavis XI*)
entries.work: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The eleventh of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Merian engraving of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) — the key of multiplication, the increase of the tincture.

## Iconography
The emblem stages augmentation: the matter, now perfected, is fed and fermented anew so that a little may colour much. The plate's figures tend a multiplying work, the tincture's virtue compounded with each repetition of the round.

## Significance
Multiplication is the penultimate office of the Stone — once the tincture is made, it is 'increased in virtue and quantity,' a grain reddening ever-greater masses. The eleventh key carries the promise that drew patrons and princes to the art: that the [[motif:philosophers stone|Stone]] is not spent in use but grows, the reddening [[motif:coniunctio|perfected matter]] feeding upon fresh body to renew itself. The German *Duodecim Claves* lent this claim the authority of the legendary monk Basil Valentine.

## For artists & game designers
The 'multiplier' beat: a finished elixir can be re-cooked with common stock to yield several doses — risk-free scaling once mastery is reached.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *Duodecim Claves*, in Michael Maier's *Tripus Aureus* (1618); engr. M. Merian in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).

entries.title: The Twelfth Key of Basil Valentine (*Douzième Clef* / *Clavis XII*)
entries.work: The Twelve Keys of Basil Valentine
entries.creator: Matthäus Merian, after Michael Maier, after Basilius Valentinus
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The twelfth and last of the *Twelve Keys* of Basilius Valentinus, in the Merian engraving of the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678) — the key of completion, the Stone achieved and enthroned.

## Iconography
The final emblem shows the work crowned: a triumphant king or red lion, the perfected matter enthroned and at rest, the round of the keys closed upon its goal. The plate gathers the series' promises into a single image of sovereignty attained — the tincture ready for projection.

## Significance
The twelfth key is the *finis operis*: the [[motif:philosophers stone|Philosophers' Stone]] complete, fixed, multiplied, and fit to transmute. The crowned figure restates the opening riddle of the series — the king who had to be killed, washed and reborn now reigns in incorruptible health. Closing the *Duodecim Claves*, this plate became one of the canonical images of the finished Great Work for seventeenth-century readers of the *Musaeum Hermeticum*.

## For artists & game designers
The 'final unlock' beat: the twelfth and last reagent completes the set and crowns the player's vessel — the long recipe resolves into a single mastered, repeatable artifact.

citations.text: Basilius Valentinus, *Duodecim Claves*, in Michael Maier's *Tripus Aureus* (1618); engr. M. Merian in the *Musaeum Hermeticum* (1678).
